stimuli.description: Red leftward arrow displayed at SSD on a left-go stop trial (signals to withhold the response).
Red rightward arrow displayed at SSD on a right-go stop trial (signals to withhold the response).
White leftward arrow with black border. Correct response is ArrowLeft.
White rightward arrow with black border. Correct response is ArrowRight.

phase_detection.complete: (!!(document.body && document.body.innerText && document.body.innerText.indexOf('Thank you for your participation') !== -1))
phase_detection.feedback: (!!(document.body && document.body.innerText && (document.body.innerText.indexOf('correct response') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('incorrect response') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('too slow') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('too fast') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('correct stop') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('remember: try to stop') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('GO TRIALS') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('Average response time') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('Press space to begin') !== -1 || document.body.innerText.indexOf('Get ready') !== -1)))

timing.trial_context_js: ((typeof block_ind !== 'undefined' && typeof trial_ind !== 'undefined') ? ('block_' + block_ind + '_trial_' + trial_ind + '_SSD_' + (typeof SSD !== 'undefined' ? SSD : 'na')) : '')

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, Beste C, Bissett PG, Brockett AT, Brown JW, Chamberlain SR, Chambers CD, Colonius H, Colzato LS, Corneil BD, Coxon JP, Dupuis A, Eagle DM, Garavan H, Greenhouse I, Heathcote A, Huster RJ, Jahfari S, Kenemans JL, Leunissen I, Li C-SR, Logan GD, Matzke D, Morein-Zamir S, Murthy A, Pare M, Poldrack RA, Ridderinkhof KR, Robbins TW, Roesch M, Rubia K, Schachar RJ, Schall JD, Stock A-K, Swann NC, Thakkar KN, van der Molen MW, Vermeylen L, Vink M, Wessel JR, Whelan R, Zandbelt BB, Boehler CN
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: review reports go-RT means in the ~450–600 ms range for healthy young adults across canonical stop-signal implementations.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Paraphrased: in two-choice RT tasks, ex-Gaussian mu commonly falls in the ~380–500 ms range with sigma ~30–80 ms and tau ~70–150 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote A, Popiel SJ, Mewhort DJK
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: ex-Gaussian decomposition yields mu as the Gaussian mode of fast responses, well-separated from tau which captures slow-tail attentional lapses.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Paraphrased: sigma estimates across speeded two-choice tasks typically fall between ~30 and ~80 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote A, Popiel SJ, Mewhort DJK
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: Gaussian-component dispersion in choice RT is narrow relative to the exponential tail.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Paraphrased: tau in two-choice RT typically spans ~70–150 ms and indexes the right-tail slow component attributable to attentional lapses or non-decision noise.

citations.authors: Ratcliff R
citations.title: Group reaction time distributions and an analysis of distribution statistics
citations.quote: Paraphrased: choice RT distributions exhibit a long positive tail well-fit by an exponential component on the order of 80–150 ms.

go_left.rationale: Two-choice arrow-direction go RTs in healthy young adults under speeded stop-signal instructions cluster around mean 540-560 ms with strong positive skew. Ex-Gaussian is the canonical fit for stop-signal go-RT distributions: mu represents the mode of the leading Gaussian component, sigma its dispersion, and tau the exponential tail driven by occasional attentional lapses.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: standard STOP-IT instructions yield comparable go-RT distributions for left and right responses in counterbalanced two-choice arrow designs; no systematic side asymmetry is reported.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Paraphrased: mu ranges of ~380–500 ms are typical for choice RT across response options.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Paraphrased: sigma in two-choice tasks ~30–80 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Paraphrased: tau range ~70–150 ms across two-choice tasks.

go_right.rationale: Mirror condition of go_left. No documented systematic hand/side asymmetry for index-finger arrow-key responses in within-subject designs; same ex-Gaussian parameters assumed and any individual side biases absorbed into between_subject_jitter.

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Paraphrased: signal-respond RTs are systematically shorter than no-signal go RTs because trials on which the stop process loses tend to be those on which the go process was fastest.

citations.authors: Band GPH, van der Molen MW, Logan GD
citations.title: Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure
citations.quote: Paraphrased: simulated and empirical signal-respond RT distributions are shifted left of and have reduced spread relative to the no-signal go distribution.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: violations of the assumption that signal-respond RT is faster than no-signal RT should be flagged as data-quality failures.

citations.authors: Band GPH, van der Molen MW, Logan GD
citations.title: Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure
citations.quote: Paraphrased: signal-respond RT distributions show compressed dispersion relative to the no-signal go distribution due to censoring of the slow tail.

citations.authors: Band GPH, van der Molen MW, Logan GD
citations.title: Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure
citations.quote: Paraphrased: the exponential tail of the signal-respond distribution is preferentially truncated because slow go processes are more often beaten by the stop process.

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Paraphrased: under the horse-race model the right tail of the go distribution is selectively removed from signal-respond trials.

stop_signal.rationale: Signal-respond RTs on commission-error stop trials are systematically shorter than the no-signal go-RT distribution — a direct prediction of the horse-race model: when the stop process loses, it tends to lose to the faster trials in the go distribution. Both mu and tau are compressed relative to go conditions, since slow tail trials are preferentially inhibited.

citations.authors: Gilden DL
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Paraphrased: serial dependence at short lags in choice-RT timeseries is pervasive across speeded paradigms.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers E-J, Farrell S, Ratcliff R
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/fα noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Paraphrased: lag-1 autocorrelations in two-choice RT timeseries fall in the range ~0.10–0.25 across studies.

autocorrelation.rationale: Lag-1 RT autocorrelations of ~0.15-0.25 are pervasive in speeded-choice paradigms and reflect short-timescale fluctuations in arousal and motor readiness. Stop-signal go-trial sequences exhibit this property in the typical range.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: canonical stop-signal sessions span roughly 5–10 minutes, below the duration at which monotonic fatigue drift dominates RT.

fatigue_drift.rationale: STOP-IT's experimental phase (~256 trials at ~1.5 s/trial ≈ 6-8 minutes) is too short for monotonic fatigue-driven RT drift to dominate. The literature on stop-signal does not single out a fatigue component above and beyond block-level vigilance variation.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: the consensus paper does not single out stimulus-repetition facilitation as a load-bearing effect in standard two-choice arrow stop-signal designs.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: stimulus-alternation cost is not emphasized as load-bearing in canonical stop-signal arrow paradigms.

condition_repetition.rationale: Stimulus-repetition effects beyond what arises from the random 8-trial basic factorial design are not emphasized in the stop-signal literature for two-choice arrow paradigms. The factorial structure balances transitions and any residual modulation is small enough that it is not load-bearing.

citations.authors: Gilden DL
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Paraphrased: 1/f fluctuations are present in cognitive RT timeseries but their amplitude is often subsumed by other sources of variability in short sessions.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers E-J, Farrell S, Ratcliff R
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/fα noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Paraphrased: pink-noise SD is small relative to lag-1 autocorrelation and ex-Gaussian tail dispersion in typical choice RT.

citations.authors: Gilden DL
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Paraphrased: 1/f^α spectra in cognitive timeseries cluster around α near 1, ranging roughly 0.8–1.2.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f fluctuations exist in RT timeseries across many speeded tasks, but the stop-signal literature does not single them out as a separate explanatory mechanism above the ex-Gaussian tail and lag-1 autocorrelation. Leaving disabled avoids double-counting slow drift variance.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: standard two-choice arrow stop-signal designs do not contain a congruency manipulation, so trial-pair conflict-modulation patterns do not apply.

citations.authors: Schmidt JR, De Houwer J
citations.title: Now you see it, now you don't: Controlling for contingencies and stimulus-repetitions eliminates the Gratton effect
citations.quote: Paraphrased: post-error trials are conventionally excluded when estimating sequential modulation because they conflate post-error slowing with sequence-driven adjustments.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Two-choice arrow stop-signal has no congruency manipulation, so the lag-1 pairwise condition-by-condition modulation pattern (which captures conflict-sequence effects in conflict paradigms) does not apply. Stop-trial sequence effects are captured by post_event_slowing instead.

citations.authors: Bissett PG, Logan GD
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Paraphrased: go-RT is slower on trials following stop-signal trials (regardless of inhibition outcome), with magnitudes on the order of tens of milliseconds in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Bissett PG, Logan GD
citations.title: Post-stop-signal slowing: Strategies dominate reflexes and implicit learning
citations.quote: Paraphrased: post-stop slowing in healthy adult samples typically falls in the ~20–60 ms range and is modulated by participants' strategic stance.

citations.authors: Rabbitt PMA
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Paraphrased: responses on trials following an error are systematically slower than responses on trials following a correct response.

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HLJ, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Paraphrased: across choice-RT tasks, post-error slowing in healthy adults typically falls in the ~20–60 ms range when measured with robust pairwise contrasts.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-stop slowing — slower go RT on the trial following any stop-signal trial, regardless of inhibition outcome — is one of the most robust findings in the stop-signal literature, with magnitudes routinely in the 25-55 ms range for healthy adults. A distinct post-commission-error adjustment, ~30-60 ms, layers on top when a stop trial elicits a leaked response. The exclusive_with_prior_triggers flag on the error trigger prevents the bot from double-applying slowing when both an interrupt and an error originate from the same prior trial.

citations.authors: Logan GD
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Paraphrased: skill acquisition follows a power function with the largest RT decrements concentrated in the earliest trials of practice.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: a short practice block is recommended to allow participants to acquire the dual-task set before the experimental phase begins.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: practice-block performance is expected to approach asymptote by the start of the experimental blocks in canonical STOP-IT.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: STOP-IT default practice block consists of 4 repetitions of the 8-trial basic factorial design, i.e., 32 practice trials.

citations.authors: Logan GD
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Paraphrased: power-law exponents for early-trial learning of choice tasks typically reach near-asymptotic performance within tens of trials.

practice_effect.rationale: Healthy adults show a brief warm-up over the practice block of stop-signal tasks, reflecting acquisition of the dual-task set (respond quickly to go AND withhold on signal). STOP-IT's 32-trial practice block is sufficient for warm-up to approach asymptote before experimental block 1. Decay_rate 0.6 yields near-asymptotic performance by the end of practice.

citations.authors: Warm JS, Parasuraman R, Matthews G
citations.title: Vigilance requires hard mental work and is stressful
citations.quote: Paraphrased: classic vigilance decrement emerges on timescales of 20+ minutes; short cognitive-control sessions are typically below this threshold.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: STOP-IT's total experimental duration (~6-8 minutes) is well below the threshold at which vigilance decrement becomes a dominant source of within-session RT variability. The vigilance literature (PVT, MRT) operates on 20+ minute scales; stop-signal does not emphasize it as a load-bearing mechanism.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Healthy-adult between-subject SD of mean go RT in stop-signal tasks is ~50-70 ms; condition-specific RT differences (signal-respond vs no-signal) vary less between subjects (~20-30 ms) because the staircased SSD couples go and signal-respond distributions. sigma_tau_range allows ~15% per-subject variation in distributional shape. accuracy_clip_range and omission_clip_range are widened beyond the typical healthy-adult go-trial band ([0.88, 1.0], [0.0, 0.06]) because the stop_signal condition targets ~50% successful inhibition by design — narrow clipping would distort stop-trial performance.

citations.authors: Congdon E, Mumford JA, Cohen JR, Galvan A, Canli T, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Paraphrased: across healthy-adult samples, between-subject SD of mean go-RT in stop-signal tasks falls in the ~50–80 ms range.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: between-subject variability in go-RT is comparable to other speeded-choice paradigms when standard inclusion criteria are applied.

citations.authors: Congdon E, Mumford JA, Cohen JR, Galvan A, Canli T, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Paraphrased: within-task condition-difference variability is smaller than overall mean-RT variability because conditions share most non-decision components.

citations.authors: Band GPH, van der Molen MW, Logan GD
citations.title: Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure
citations.quote: Paraphrased: staircased SSD couples go and signal-respond distributions, reducing between-subject variability in their difference.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Paraphrased: individual-subject estimates of ex-Gaussian sigma and tau vary by ~±15–30% around the group central tendency.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: go-trial accuracy is expected to remain near ceiling (>90%) in healthy adults, with between-subject SD on the order of a few percentage points.

citations.authors: Congdon E, Mumford JA, Cohen JR, Galvan A, Canli T, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Paraphrased: go-trial accuracy SD across healthy subjects is small (~0.01–0.04).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: go-trial omission rates above ~10% are flagged as data-quality concerns; between-subject variability in omissions is small in compliant samples.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: data-quality guidance recommends inhibition rates be close to 50% by design while go-accuracy should remain high; clipping bounds should accommodate the staircase-targeted ~50% stop accuracy.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Paraphrased: by design the stop-trial omission rate is centered on ~50% under a 1-up/1-down staircase, so admissible omission ranges must accommodate this target.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'STOP-IT (Stop-Signal Task, jsPsych version)' with 4 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 3 conditions; enabled 3 temporal effects.
Produced 47 citations across 30 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 37 parameters; 7 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed after 1 refinement(s): 22 trials matched, conditions ['go_left', 'go_right', 'stop_signal'] all observed. See pilot_refinement_*.diff for changes the bot made.
